What’s in Motion Description: Districts receive targeted funding to support students who need interventions — tutoring, transportation, mentoring — to cross the graduation finish line.
Schools use data-driven indicators to identify students at risk of not graduating and connect them with support services early.
High school students take a dedicated preparation course before the ACT, building the skills and confidence they need to score higher.
Instructional coaches work alongside teachers to strengthen daily lessons in reading, writing, and math — the foundations students need for the ACT.
Schools expand access to Advanced Placement, International Baccalaureate, and other rigorous courses so every student can take on a college-level challenge.
Coaches help teachers refine their instruction so more students are prepared to take on the challenge of Advanced Placement courses.
A shared framework sets clear expectations for high-quality instruction, so teachers can deliver the rigorous lessons that help AP students pass the exam.
New standards for teacher training ensure educators get the high-quality preparation they need to help students master AP material.
Programs like NC College Connect and NextNC give students a direct path to enroll in college courses while still in high school.
Communities, businesses, and families get toolkits to guide students in planning their path — including enrolling in college courses before graduation.
High-quality online content and personalized digital tools give students the support to succeed in college-level courses and earn the credit.
Schools share proven strategies to improve attendance — because students who show up are far more likely to earn their credit.
Students create personalized plans mapping the courses, credentials, and experiences that connect their time in school to their career goals.
Businesses and community groups invest in innovative school programs — bringing funding and real-world partnerships that support career learning.
Career and technical programs are growing — giving more students the chance to earn the industry credentials that employers value.
Education networks connect schools to share what works — spreading the practices that help more students earn the credentials employers want.
Schools adopt research-based reading instruction, giving children the strongest foundation for learning to read by fourth grade.
A statewide campaign puts 10 million books in students’ hands each year — growing a love of reading that powers learning.
Research-based reading practices extend through middle school, so eighth-graders build the comprehension skills they need for every subject.
Districts adopt best practices to limit wireless device distractions, giving students more focused time to read, learn, and think deeply.
The Golden LEAF Schools Initiative brings high-quality math instruction to rural schools, helping children develop strong early math skills.
A virtual academy gives families short, accessible online sessions on how to support their child’s learning at home — including early math.
The Golden LEAF Schools Initiative transforms math instruction in rural schools through a proven model, raising expectations and results.
New progress checks give teachers timely data on what students know — so they can adjust math instruction before it’s too late.
A statewide toolkit helps schools teach values like respect, responsibility, and perseverance — the character traits that shape good citizens.
Schools create supportive environments where every student feels they belong — fostering the positive climate where strong character grows.
Educators and community partners share stories of student achievement and school success — showing families the value of their public schools.
Communities across North Carolina share how public education shapes their neighborhoods and prepares children for the future.
